places.name: Versailles Restaurant
places.reviewSummary: The self-proclaimed 'world's most famous Cuban restaurant' and the unofficial town square of Cuban Miami. Go for ropa vieja, lechon asado, and a cortadito at the walk-up window.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the authentic Cuban classics and lively atmosphere, though some note it can be touristy and crowded at peak hours.
places.localTip: Skip the dining room wait and order a cortadito and pastelito at the ventanita (window) out front, where locals talk politics.
places.story: Opened in 1971 by a Cuban exile who named it after the French palace, Versailles became the living room of Miami's exile community, where presidential candidates still stop to court votes. When Fidel Castro died in 2016, thousands poured into this parking lot to celebrate until dawn.
places.highlight: Don't miss the ventanita cortadito

places.name: Joe's Stone Crab
places.reviewSummary: A century-old Miami Beach institution famous for chilled stone crab claws with mustard sauce, hash browns, and key lime pie. A bucket-list seafood meal.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners rave about the stone crab and old-school service, with frequent caveats about long no-reservation waits and high prices.
places.localTip: They don't take reservations for the main dining room and lines are brutal; order takeout next door at Joe's Take Away to skip the wait.
places.story: Joe Weiss started selling fish from his Miami Beach porch in 1913, then discovered locals would pay for the stone crab claws nobody else wanted. Over a century later it's still family-run, and the claws are still harvested sustainably so the crab can regrow its claw and live on.
places.highlight: Don't miss the key lime pie

places.name: MILA
places.reviewSummary: A glamorous rooftop restaurant blending Mediterranean and Asian flavors, known for wagyu, sushi, and a buzzy late-night scene. Dress up.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the inventive 'Mediterrasian' menu and rooftop vibe, though some find it pricey and more scene than substance.
places.localTip: Ask for a table on the open-air terrace rather than the indoor lounge for the full rooftop experience.
places.story: MILA opened in 2020 atop a 1930s building and quickly became one of South Beach's most photographed dining rooms. Its name nods to the fusion of Milan and Asia, and the kitchen leans hard into theatrical plates meant to be shared.

places.name: La Mar by Gaston Acurio
places.reviewSummary: Star chef Gaston Acurio's Peruvian flagship on Biscayne Bay, celebrated for ceviche, tiradito, and pisco sours with skyline views. A special-occasion favorite.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers consistently praise the ceviche and bay views, with occasional notes that it gets loud and reservations are essential.
places.localTip: Come for the waterfront terrace at sunset and order the ceviche sampler before the dinner rush.
places.story: Peruvian chef Gaston Acurio brought his globally acclaimed La Mar concept to Brickell Key, perched right on the water. The restaurant helped cement Miami's reputation as a serious Latin American dining capital beyond just Cuban food.

places.name: Garcia's Seafood Grille & Fish Market
places.address: 398 NW North River Dr, Miami, FL 33128
places.reviewSummary: A no-frills family fish house on the working Miami River serving just-off-the-boat conch fritters, stone crab, and fish sandwiches on a breezy deck.
places.googleReviewSummary: Locals love the fresh-off-the-boat seafood and river views, though some mention slow service when it's packed on weekends.
places.localTip: Sit on the upstairs deck and watch the fishing boats and yachts pass; cash-friendly and worth the wait.
places.story: The Garcia family has fished these waters for generations, running a wholesale fish market on the Miami River before opening this beloved grille. It's one of the last authentic working-river restaurants in a city that's rapidly gone glossy.
places.highlight: Don't miss the conch fritters

places.name: Chef Creole Seasoned Kitchen
places.reviewSummary: A beloved Little Haiti counter serving griot (fried pork), conch, fried snapper, and plantains. Generous, garlicky, and deeply authentic.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the griot and fresh seafood, with some noting it's takeout-focused with limited seating.
places.localTip: Order the griot with pikliz (spicy slaw) and a side of fried plantains; portions are big enough to split.
places.story: Chef Wilkinson 'Ken' Sejour built Chef Creole into a Miami institution celebrating Haitian flavors long before Little Haiti was on any food map. It's the kind of place where the conch is fried to order and the line is full of regulars.

places.name: Big Pink
places.reviewSummary: A pink-soaked American comfort-food diner in South of Fifth famous for huge portions, TV dinners on metal trays, and late-night crowds.
places.googleReviewSummary: Diners love the playful retro vibe and big portions, though a few mention it can be noisy and hit-or-miss on busy nights.
places.localTip: It's open late and a classic post-club stop; the TV dinner sampler is built for sharing after a night out.
places.story: Big Pink has been South Beach's favorite oversized diner since the late '90s, a campy pink playground where club kids and families collide at 3am. The cheeky menu and metal-tray TV dinners are pure Miami theatrics.

places.name: Cvi.che 105
places.reviewSummary: A vibrant downtown Peruvian spot known for inventive ceviches, lomo saltado, and pisco cocktails. A reliable, lively crowd-pleaser.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the fresh, creative ceviches and energetic atmosphere, with some noting it can get loud and crowded.
places.localTip: Try the ceviche mixto and the tiradito; portions are generous so come hungry or share.
places.story: Chef Juan Chipoco turned Cvi.che 105 into one of downtown Miami's most beloved Peruvian destinations, helping spark the city's ceviche craze. The energy here is pure Lima-meets-Miami.

places.name: Panther Coffee
places.reviewSummary: Miami's pioneering small-batch roaster, beloved for pour-overs, espresso, and pastries in a sunny, art-filled Wynwood space.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the high-quality, locally roasted coffee and relaxed vibe, with occasional notes about limited seating and parking.
places.localTip: Grab a cold brew and a pastry and sit on the patio to watch Wynwood's mural crowd roll by.
places.story: Panther Coffee, founded by Joel and Leticia Pollock, kicked off Miami's third-wave coffee movement when it opened in Wynwood in 2010. It roasts beans on-site and helped turn an industrial district into a creative hub.

places.name: Cafe La Trova
places.reviewSummary: Award-winning Cuban cafe and cantinero cocktail bar from chef Michelle Bernstein and bartender Julio Cabrera, with live music and classic Cuban dishes.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the cocktails, food, and live music, though some say it gets crowded and loud on weekend nights.
places.localTip: Come early for a cortadito and croquetas, or stay late for the cantinero-style daiquiris and son cubano band.
places.story: Cafe La Trova revives the lost art of the Cuban cantinero, bartenders who mix and dance to live music. It's earned national acclaim while staying rooted in Calle Ocho's soul.
places.highlight: Don't miss a classic daiquiri

places.name: Vicky Bakery
places.reviewSummary: A family-run Cuban bakery chain famous for guava pastelitos, croquetas, and strong cafe con leche. A local breakfast ritual.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the fresh pastelitos and affordable Cuban coffee, with some noting morning lines move fast but get busy.
places.localTip: Order a dozen pastelitos de guayaba y queso to go; they're the city's classic office-meeting offering.
places.story: Vicky Bakery started as a single Hialeah panaderia in 1972 and grew into a Miami institution beloved across generations of Cuban families. The guava-and-cheese pastelito here is a hometown rite of passage.

places.name: OTL (Order to Live)
places.reviewSummary: A bright, design-forward all-day cafe known for espresso, healthy brunch plates, and a relaxed remote-work crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests praise the coffee and airy space, with some mentioning it gets busy with laptops on weekday mornings.
places.localTip: Great for working; grab the avocado toast and a flat white before the brunch rush hits.
places.story: OTL became an Edgewater favorite as the neighborhood transformed from warehouses into a residential hub. Its minimalist, plant-filled space made it a go-to for Miami's growing creative-class crowd.

places.name: Azucar Ice Cream Company
places.reviewSummary: A whimsical Calle Ocho ice cream shop serving Cuban-inspired flavors like 'Abuela Maria' (guava, cream cheese, Maria cookies) under a giant ice cream cone sign.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers adore the unique Cuban-inspired flavors and friendly service, with some noting it's cash-and-card busy on weekends.
places.localTip: Order the Abuela Maria flavor; it tastes exactly like a Cuban grandmother's dessert.
places.story: Founder Suzy Batlle named her flavors after Cuban memories, turning childhood desserts into scoops. The giant cone sign out front has become a Calle Ocho photo landmark.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Abuela Maria

places.name: All Day
places.reviewSummary: A sleek downtown coffee bar and cafe praised for excellent espresso, breakfast bowls, and a calm, design-minded atmosphere.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the quality coffee and minimalist space, though some find it small and busy at peak hours.
places.localTip: The breakfast served all day is the draw; try the soft scrambled eggs with a cappuccino.
places.story: All Day helped anchor downtown Miami's coffee renaissance with carefully sourced beans and an all-day breakfast ethos. It's a quiet, design-driven counterpoint to the city's flashier scenes.

places.name: Vizcaya Museum and Gardens
places.reviewSummary: An opulent 1916 Italian Renaissance-style villa on Biscayne Bay with formal gardens, fountains, and a stone barge. Miami's most photogenic estate.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it stunning and worth every penny, with frequent notes about heat, mosquitoes, and the need to buy timed tickets ahead.
places.localTip: Buy timed tickets online and arrive at opening to beat the heat and crowds in the gardens.
places.story: Industrialist James Deering built Vizcaya as a winter retreat in 1916, importing antiques and even a stone breakwater barge sculpted by Stirling Calder. The gardens were designed to look centuries old the day they opened.
places.highlight: Don't miss the stone barge in the bay

places.name: Wynwood Walls
places.reviewSummary: An open-air museum of large-scale murals by world-renowned street artists, the anchor of Wynwood's arts district. Endlessly Instagrammable.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the world-class murals and vibrant energy, though some feel the paid entry is unnecessary when free street art surrounds it.
places.localTip: The surrounding streets are covered in free murals too; explore beyond the paid walls for hidden gems.
places.story: Developer Tony Goldman launched Wynwood Walls in 2009 to turn blank warehouse facades into a curated street-art destination. It transformed a forgotten industrial zone into one of the world's most influential mural districts.

places.name: Art Deco Historic District
places.reviewSummary: The largest concentration of 1930s Art Deco architecture in the world, lining Ocean Drive and Collins Avenue with pastel hotels and neon. A walkable open-air gallery.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the colorful architecture and beachfront energy, with frequent notes that Ocean Drive itself is touristy and pushy with restaurant hawkers.
places.localTip: Take the Art Deco Welcome Center's guided walking tour, then come back at dusk when the neon lights up.
places.story: When developers wanted to bulldoze South Beach's faded hotels in the 1970s, activist Barbara Capitman led a fight to save them, creating the nation's first 20th-century historic district. Those pastel facades and neon signs became Miami's signature look.
places.highlight: Don't miss Ocean Drive's neon at night

places.name: Bayside Marketplace
places.reviewSummary: A festive waterfront shopping and dining complex on Biscayne Bay, the launch point for skyline boat tours and a lively live-music spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the bay views, boat tours, and live music, though some find the shopping touristy and parking pricey.
places.localTip: Use it as the launch pad for a Biscayne Bay boat tour past Star Island's celebrity mansions.
places.story: Bayside opened in 1987 as downtown Miami's waterfront playground, mixing shops, street performers, and dock-side boats. It's the easiest place in the city to hop aboard a bay cruise.

places.name: Phillip and Patricia Frost Museum of Science
places.reviewSummary: A modern science museum with a planetarium, a three-level aquarium, and hands-on exhibits. Excellent for families and a rainy-day backup.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the aquarium and planetarium, with some noting it's pricey and best for kids over a full day.
places.localTip: Look up through the donut-shaped Gulf Stream aquarium tank from below for the best view of the sharks and rays.
places.story: Opened in 2017 in Museum Park, the Frost Science features a 500,000-gallon aquarium shaped like a cone so visitors see marine life from multiple angles. Its planetarium projects onto a tilted 250-degree dome for an immersive sky.
places.highlight: Don't miss the planetarium show

places.name: Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden
places.address: 10901 Old Cutler Rd, Coral Gables, FL 33156
places.reviewSummary: An 83-acre tropical paradise of palms, rare flowering trees, a rainforest, and butterfly conservatory south of the city. Serene and lush.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the lush, peaceful grounds and seasonal exhibits, with some noting it's spread out and hot to walk in summer.
places.localTip: Check the calendar for the annual Chihuly glass installations or the chocolate and mango festivals.
places.story: Founded in 1938 and named for plant explorer David Fairchild, the garden holds one of the world's great collections of tropical plants. Researchers here work to conserve endangered species from around the globe.

places.name: The Barnacle Historic State Park
places.reviewSummary: The oldest house in Miami-Dade still on its original site, a 19th-century bayfront home set in a hardwood hammock. A peaceful glimpse of pioneer Miami.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers find it a tranquil, history-rich escape, though some note limited hours and a small footprint.
places.localTip: Come for the monthly moonlight concerts on the bayfront lawn under the trees.
places.story: Built in 1891 by Commodore Ralph Munroe, the Barnacle's clever roof was designed to catch bay breezes before air conditioning. It survives as a green pocket of old Coconut Grove amid the modern city.

places.name: Freedom Tower
places.reviewSummary: A 1925 Mediterranean Revival landmark, dubbed the 'Ellis Island of the South' for processing Cuban refugees in the 1960s. Now a museum and cultural center.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers value its powerful immigration history and architecture, though some note exhibits and access can vary with renovations.
places.localTip: Light it up in your photos at night when the tower glows; check ahead as it periodically closes for restoration.
places.story: Modeled after the Giralda bell tower in Seville, the Freedom Tower welcomed hundreds of thousands of Cuban exiles fleeing Castro. For many Cuban-Americans it's a sacred symbol of arrival and freedom.

places.name: Matheson Hammock Park
places.address: 9610 Old Cutler Rd, Coral Gables, FL 33156
places.reviewSummary: A locals' favorite park with a man-made atoll pool that fills with tides, a marina, and a skyline view. Calm, family-friendly swimming.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the calm atoll pool and skyline views, though some mention seaweed and crowds on weekends.
places.localTip: The tide-flushed atoll pool is calmest and clearest at high tide; come on a weekday morning.
places.story: Built in the 1930s by the Civilian Conservation Corps, Matheson's circular atoll pool uses Biscayne Bay's tides to refresh itself. It remains a quiet local antidote to the South Beach crowds.

places.name: Robert Is Here Fruit Stand
places.reviewSummary: A legendary roadside farm stand near the Everglades famous for tropical fruit milkshakes (key lime, mamey, soursop) and exotic produce.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers rave about the fresh fruit shakes and friendly farm vibe, with some noting it's a drive and gets busy on weekends.
places.localTip: It's the perfect stop on the way to the Everglades or Keys; the key lime milkshake is the must-order.
places.story: Robert started selling cucumbers at this corner as a six-year-old in 1959, and the hand-painted sign stuck. Today his farm stand is a pilgrimage for fruit lovers heading toward the Keys.
places.highlight: Don't miss the key lime shake

places.name: Espanola Way
places.reviewSummary: A charming Mediterranean-style pedestrian lane of pastel buildings, sidewalk cafes, and string lights. A romantic break from Ocean Drive.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the European feel and outdoor dining, though some find restaurant hosts pushy and prices touristy.
places.localTip: Visit in the early evening for tapas and live flamenco when the string lights come on.
places.story: Built in 1925 as a Spanish-village 'artists' colony,' Espanola Way once hosted Al Capone's gambling parlors and a young Desi Arnaz drumming the conga. It's been lovingly restored into a pedestrian charmer.

places.name: Key Biscayne / Crandon Park
places.reviewSummary: A wide, calm public beach on a barrier island just minutes from downtown, with palm groves, picnic spots, and skyline views back to the city.
places.googleReviewSummary: Beachgoers praise the clean sand and family-friendly calm water, with some noting parking fills early on weekends.
places.localTip: Cross the Rickenbacker Causeway early; the beach is calmer and less crowded than South Beach.
places.story: Crandon Park was once a coconut plantation donated to the county in the 1940s with the condition it become a park. The island's causeway offers one of Miami's most beloved skyline-and-water drives.

places.name: Maximo Gomez Park (Domino Park)
places.reviewSummary: A tiny corner park on Calle Ocho where Cuban elders gather daily to play dominoes and argue politics under the trees. Pure Little Havana.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors find it authentic and atmospheric, though some note it's small and players are there to play, not perform.
places.localTip: Watch respectfully from the edges; the games are for regulars, but the cigar-scented energy is the real attraction.
places.story: Named for a Cuban independence hero, Domino Park has been the social heart of Cuban Miami for decades. The slap of dominoes and clouds of cigar smoke here are the soundtrack of Calle Ocho.

places.name: The Citadel
places.reviewSummary: A stylish multi-vendor food hall and rooftop bar in up-and-coming Little River, mixing global street food, coffee, and cocktails.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the variety of vendors and rooftop bar, with some noting it gets crowded and parking is tight at peak times.
places.localTip: Head to the rooftop bar at golden hour for a drink with a skyline view away from the tourist zones.
places.story: The Citadel converted a former bank building into a buzzing food hall that helped put Little River on Miami's map. It's a snapshot of the city's restless creative migration north.

places.name: Biscayne Bay Skyline Cruise
places.reviewSummary: A narrated boat cruise from Bayside past the downtown skyline, Port of Miami cruise ships, and Star Island's celebrity mansions.
places.googleReviewSummary: Riders enjoy the skyline and mansion views, though some say the celebrity-home narration is exaggerated.
places.localTip: Book the sunset departure for the best light on the skyline and the cruise ships heading out.
places.story: These bay cruises have shown off Miami's waterfront for generations, pointing out the manmade Star and Palm Islands dredged up in the 1920s land boom. The skyline they pass has exploded with condos in just two decades.

places.name: Everglades Safari Park Airboat Tours
places.reviewSummary: A classic airboat ride into the Everglades sawgrass to spot alligators, plus a wildlife show. A quintessential South Florida day trip.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the thrill of the airboat and seeing gators, with some noting the gator show feels touristy.
places.localTip: Go on the first tour of the morning when wildlife is most active and the heat is bearable.
places.story: Just 40 minutes west of downtown, the River of Grass stretches to the horizon, the only place on Earth where alligators and crocodiles coexist. Airboats were originally built by Glades hunters to skim over the shallow marsh.
places.highlight: Don't miss the alligator sightings

places.name: Biscayne National Park
places.reviewSummary: A national park that's 95% water, protecting coral reefs, mangroves, and shipwrecks. Snorkel, kayak, or boat across turquoise Biscayne Bay.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers call it a hidden gem with gorgeous water, though some note you really need a boat tour to experience it.
places.localTip: Book a ranger-led snorkel or the Maritime Heritage Trail boat trip to actually reach the reefs and wrecks.
places.story: Biscayne became a national park in 1980 to protect the northernmost Florida Keys reefs from development. Beneath the surface lies a 'shipwreck trail' of sunken vessels you can snorkel right over.

places.name: Little Havana Food & Walking Tour
places.address: Calle Ocho, SW 8th St, Miami, FL 33135
places.reviewSummary: A guided tasting walk through Calle Ocho with stops for Cuban coffee, croquetas, cigars, and fresh fruit, plus neighborhood history.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about knowledgeable guides and generous tastings, with few complaints beyond the heat.
places.localTip: Book a morning tour before it gets hot, and come hungry; the tastings add up to a meal.
places.story: These walking tours weave through the cafes, cigar shops, and murals that make Calle Ocho the cultural spine of Cuban America. Guides share exile stories you'd never get from a guidebook.

places.name: El Titan de Bronze Cigar Factory
places.reviewSummary: A working boutique cigar factory on Calle Ocho where you can watch master rollers (torcedores) hand-make premium cigars and buy on the spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love watching the rollers and the quality cigars, with some noting it's small and best for cigar enthusiasts.
places.localTip: Chat with the rollers, who learned the craft in Cuba; they'll recommend a stick to suit a first-timer.
places.story: El Titan de Bronze has hand-rolled cigars on Calle Ocho since 1995, employing Cuban-trained torcedores who follow traditional methods. Watching a roller shape a cigar from raw leaf is a vanishing art.

places.name: 1 Hotel South Beach Rooftop
places.reviewSummary: An adults-only rooftop pool and bar atop an eco-luxury hotel, with sweeping ocean views and a chic, plant-draped vibe.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the views and design, though some note it's pricey and access can be limited to hotel guests at busy times.
places.localTip: Visit at sunset for cocktails; non-guests can sometimes access via the rooftop bar, so call ahead.
places.story: 1 Hotel built its brand on reclaimed wood, living walls, and a sustainability ethos rare for flashy South Beach. Its rooftop is one of the most serene high-up perches on the beach.

places.name: Perez Art Museum Miami (PAMM)
places.reviewSummary: Miami's flagship contemporary art museum on Biscayne Bay, designed by Herzog & de Meuron with hanging gardens and a focus on Latin American art.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the architecture, bay terrace, and rotating exhibits, with some noting the permanent collection is modest in size.
places.localTip: Go on a free 'second Saturday' or for the bayfront terrace and Verde restaurant, even without a ticket.
places.story: Opened in 2013 and named for donor Jorge Perez, PAMM's stilted design lets storm surge pass beneath it, with lush hanging gardens by botanist Patrick Blanc. Its waterfront terrace is a public gathering spot.
places.highlight: Don't miss the hanging gardens

places.name: Rubell Museum
places.reviewSummary: One of the world's largest privately owned contemporary art collections, with major works by Basquiat, Keith Haring, and emerging artists in a converted warehouse.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the bold, large-scale contemporary works and spacious galleries, with some noting it's a bit out of the way.
places.localTip: The Rubells often show artists years before they're famous; ask staff which emerging names to watch.
places.story: Mera and Don Rubell began collecting in 1964 with a modest budget and an eye for unknown talent, famously buying Keith Haring early. Their collection helped seed Miami's rise as a contemporary art capital.

places.name: Institute of Contemporary Art (ICA Miami)
places.reviewSummary: A free contemporary art museum in the Design District with a striking metallic facade and a sculpture garden. Cutting-edge and accessible.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors appreciate the free entry and provocative exhibits, with some noting it's compact and quick to tour.
places.localTip: Admission is always free; pair it with a stroll through the Design District's luxury-store art installations.
places.story: ICA Miami moved into its sculptural, grid-faceted building in 2017 and committed to free admission to keep contemporary art open to all. Its rear sculpture garden is a quiet courtyard surprise.

places.name: Bakehouse Art Complex
places.reviewSummary: A nonprofit artist-studio complex in a former 1930s bakery, with resident artists' workspaces and rotating gallery shows. A real working art hub.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers enjoy meeting working artists and the authentic studio vibe, with some noting variable hours for studio access.
places.localTip: Visit during an open-studio night to chat with resident artists and buy directly from them.
places.story: Housed in an Art Deco former industrial bakery, the Bakehouse has nurtured Miami artists since 1985. It's a grassroots counterpoint to the city's glossy commercial galleries.

places.name: Adrienne Arsht Center for the Performing Arts
places.reviewSummary: Miami's grand performing arts center, a Cesar Pelli-designed campus hosting opera, ballet, Broadway tours, and concerts.
places.googleReviewSummary: Patrons praise the acoustics and striking architecture, with some noting parking and traffic around events.
places.localTip: Check for free outdoor plaza performances and the discounted last-minute ticket programs.
places.story: Opened in 2006, the Arsht Center anchored downtown's cultural revival with two soaring halls connected by a dramatic glass canopy. It's now one of the largest performing arts centers in the country.

places.name: Miami Design District
places.reviewSummary: A luxury shopping and design neighborhood doubling as an open-air gallery, with public art, the Fly's Eye Dome, and avant-garde architecture.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors admire the architecture and public art, though many note it's geared toward high-end luxury shopping.
places.localTip: You don't need to shop; wander to spot Buckminster Fuller's Fly's Eye Dome and the hidden Palm Court installations.
places.story: Developer Craig Robins reimagined a faded furniture district into a fusion of fashion, art, and architecture, commissioning starchitects and public installations. It blurs the line between mall and museum.
places.highlight: Don't miss the Fly's Eye Dome

places.name: Art Basel Miami Beach (Convention Center)
places.neighborhood: Miami Beach Convention Center, Miami Beach
places.address: 1901 Convention Center Dr, Miami Beach, FL 33139
places.reviewSummary: The Americas' premier contemporary art fair each December, drawing global galleries, collectors, and a citywide explosion of satellite events.
places.googleReviewSummary: Attendees call it world-class and electric, with caveats about crowds, steep ticket prices, and packed early-December logistics.
places.localTip: Tickets sell out; if you can't get in, the free satellite fairs and Wynwood events deliver much of the energy.
places.story: Art Basel chose Miami Beach for its first U.S. edition in 2002, and the fair transformed the city into a global art-world capital every December. The entire city now reinvents itself around it each winter.

places.name: Ball & Chain
places.reviewSummary: A historic Calle Ocho bar and live-music venue with nightly salsa, son, and Latin jazz, plus mojitos and a pineapple-shaped stage in back.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the live bands and dancing, with some noting it gets packed and pricey on weekend nights.
places.localTip: Come on a weeknight for the best dance floor access; free salsa lessons are sometimes on offer.
places.story: Ball & Chain first opened in 1935 and hosted jazz legends like Billie Holiday and Count Basie before closing for decades. Revived in 2014, it again pulses with live Cuban music nightly under the Pineapple stage's palm fronds.
places.highlight: Don't miss the live salsa band

places.name: Le Chat Noir at The Betsy
places.reviewSummary: An intimate jazz lounge inside The Betsy Hotel, hosting nightly live jazz in a speakeasy-style room. A sophisticated, low-key night.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the talented musicians and cozy setting, with some noting limited space and a minimum spend.
places.localTip: Reserve ahead; the room is tiny and fills fast, especially on weekend jazz nights.
places.story: The Betsy Hotel champions arts and literature, hosting nightly jazz and a writers' residency. Its hidden Carlton Room lounge keeps live jazz alive on a beach better known for EDM.

places.name: Kaseya Center
places.reviewSummary: Miami's bayfront arena, home of the Miami Heat and host to major touring concerts and events. Big-name nights with a skyline backdrop.
places.googleReviewSummary: Fans praise the views and atmosphere, with common complaints about pricey parking and concessions.
places.localTip: Take the free Metromover to the Freedom Tower or Park West stops to skip the event parking crush.
places.story: Opened in 1999 on Biscayne Bay, this arena has hosted the Heat's championship runs and global superstars. Its waterfront setting makes arriving by water taxi a uniquely Miami entrance.

places.name: Hoy Como Ayer
places.reviewSummary: A small, smoky Little Havana club famous for authentic Cuban son, timba, and trova, drawing serious music fans and Cuban performers.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the authentic Cuban music and intimacy, with some noting it's tiny, cash-friendly, and gets crowded.
places.localTip: Go for the late sets when Cuban musicians and dancers really heat up; bring cash for the cover.
places.story: Hoy Como Ayer ('Today Like Yesterday') has been a hallowed stage for Cuban roots music in exile, hosting legends and the famed 'Fuacata' nights. It's where Miami's Cuban music underground gathers.

places.name: Miami Beach Bandshell
places.reviewSummary: A breezy open-air amphitheater near the ocean hosting eclectic concerts from world music to jazz to indie acts. Relaxed and community-driven.
places.googleReviewSummary: Concertgoers love the ocean-air setting and diverse lineup, with some noting limited seating and parking nearby.
places.localTip: Bring a light layer; the ocean breeze cools things off, and many shows are affordable or free.
places.story: This North Beach landmark has hosted outdoor concerts for decades and is now run by a nonprofit booking globally adventurous acts. It's one of the last places to hear live music with sand underfoot.

places.name: LIV
places.reviewSummary: Miami's most famous nightclub, inside the Fontainebleau, drawing A-list DJs and celebrities for over-the-top bottle-service nights.
places.googleReviewSummary: Partiers call it iconic with top DJs and production, with frequent caveats about expensive tables, long lines, and strict door.
places.localTip: Get on the guest list or book a table ahead; the door is notoriously selective and lines are long.
places.story: LIV opened in 2008 inside the legendary Fontainebleau and quickly became the epicenter of Miami's celebrity nightlife. Its name and excess have defined the city's club scene for over a decade.

places.name: E11EVEN Miami
places.reviewSummary: A 24-hour 'ultraclub' blending nightclub, cabaret, and live performance, open round the clock with acrobats and world-class DJs.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests rave about the production, performers, and never-closing energy, with common notes on steep prices and intense crowds.
places.localTip: It truly never closes; the after-hours stretch from sunrise onward is when it gets legendary.
places.story: E11EVEN opened in 2014 as a 24/7 entertainment complex unlike anything else in the U.S., merging Vegas-style cabaret with nightclub energy. It even launched its own cryptocurrency and hotel tower.

places.name: Sugar at EAST Miami
places.reviewSummary: A 40th-floor rooftop garden bar in Brickell with Asian-inspired cocktails, lush plants, and panoramic city-and-bay views.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the views and ambiance, with frequent notes about long waits, crowds, and pricey drinks at sunset.
places.localTip: Arrive right at opening or for sunset to grab a railing spot before the crowd and the wait build.
places.story: Sugar sits atop the EAST Miami hotel in the Brickell City Centre complex, turning a rooftop into a tropical garden in the sky. It became Brickell's signature spot for a skyline cocktail.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunset from the rail

places.name: Sweet Liberty Drinks & Supply Co.
places.reviewSummary: An award-winning, unpretentious craft cocktail bar off the beach beloved for daiquiris, happy hour, and a warm neighborhood vibe.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers praise the cocktails and friendly service, with some noting it gets packed during its popular happy hour.
places.localTip: Their Daiquiri Time Out (DTO) happy hour is one of the best deals on the beach.
places.story: Founded by the late, beloved bartender John Lermayer, Sweet Liberty won international acclaim for serious cocktails without the attitude. It remains a tribute to Miami's craft-bar coming of age.
places.highlight: Don't miss the frozen daiquiri

places.name: Story Nightclub
places.reviewSummary: A high-energy EDM nightclub in South of Fifth with major DJ residencies, dramatic production, and a younger party crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Clubbers love the DJs and lights, with frequent caveats about cost, crowds, and a tough door policy.
places.localTip: Buy tickets or table service online in advance; walking up to the door rarely works on big nights.
places.story: Story opened in 2012 as a sister venue to LIV, bringing world-class electronic acts to South Beach. Its name reflects the theatrical, ever-changing 'story' of each night's production.

places.name: Lagniappe House
places.reviewSummary: A laid-back wine bar in a converted house with a string-lit backyard garden and nightly live jazz and soul. Romantic and unpretentious.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love the backyard ambiance and live music, with some noting service can be slow when it's busy.
places.localTip: Order a cheese-and-charcuterie board and grab a backyard table for the nightly live band.
places.story: Lagniappe (a Creole word for 'a little something extra') turned an old house into one of Miami's most charming wine gardens. Its tree-shaded backyard is a beloved escape from the club scene.

places.name: Mac's Club Deuce Bar
places.reviewSummary: The oldest bar in Miami Beach, a no-frills neon-lit dive open till 5am with stiff cheap drinks and a gloriously unpretentious crowd.
places.googleReviewSummary: Reviewers love the authentic dive vibe and strong pours, with some noting it's cash-only and gritty by design.
places.localTip: It's cash-only and open till 5am; the perfect last stop when South Beach's clubs feel too polished.
places.story: Open since 1926 and run by Mac Klein for over half a century, the Deuce is a survivor of old Miami Beach. Anthony Bourdain called it one of his favorite bars in the world.
places.highlight: Don't miss the neon facade for a photo

places.name: Club Space
places.reviewSummary: Miami's legendary after-hours techno temple, famous for its open-air Terrace and marathon sunrise sets from the world's top DJs.
places.googleReviewSummary: Ravers call the Terrace and sunrise sets unmatched, with frequent notes about long lines, high cost, and no-phone-photo zones.
places.localTip: The magic is the sunrise on the Terrace; pace yourself and stay until the sun comes up.
places.story: Club Space pioneered Miami's 24-hour-permit after-hours scene, and its open-air Terrace at sunrise is now a global techno pilgrimage. DJs routinely play sets that stretch past noon the next day.
places.highlight: Don't miss sunrise on the Terrace

places.name: Miami Culinary Tours Cooking Class
places.reviewSummary: Hands-on classes teaching Cuban and Latin dishes like ropa vieja, empanadas, and mojitos, led by local chefs. Fun and approachable.
places.googleReviewSummary: Participants praise the engaging chefs and generous tastings, with few complaints beyond small class sizes filling fast.
places.localTip: Book the mojito-and-empanada class for a hands-on intro to Cuban flavors you can recreate at home.
places.story: This operator grew from Miami's popular food tours into immersive cooking classes celebrating the city's Latin heritage. You leave able to roll an empanada and muddle a proper mojito.

places.name: Ceramic League of Miami
places.reviewSummary: A nonprofit ceramics studio offering wheel-throwing and hand-building classes and open studio time in a leafy setting. Welcoming to beginners.
places.googleReviewSummary: Students love the supportive instructors and community feel, with some noting classes book up and the location is south of the city.
places.localTip: Sign up early for the beginner wheel class; sessions are popular and fill quickly.
places.story: Founded in 1953, the Ceramic League is one of the oldest arts nonprofits in Miami, run by and for clay artists. Its workshops have introduced generations of locals to the pottery wheel.

places.name: Cafe La Trova Cantinero Experience
places.reviewSummary: A cocktail workshop in the art of the Cuban cantinero, learning to mix and showman-shake classic daiquiris and mojitos with master bartenders.
places.googleReviewSummary: Guests love learning the theatrical cantinero technique and tasting their creations, with some noting it's a premium-priced experience.
places.localTip: Book directly with the bar for private cantinero sessions; weekday afternoons are easiest to arrange.
places.story: Cafe La Trova revived the nearly lost Cuban cantinero tradition of bartenders who mix with flair and rhythm. Learning the technique here is a rare hands-on link to pre-revolution Havana bar culture.

places.name: Virginia Key Outdoor Center
places.reviewSummary: An eco-focused outdoor center offering kayak and paddleboard rentals and guided mangrove tours through calm island waters.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors praise the friendly staff and serene mangrove paddles, with some noting limited hours and weather-dependent conditions.
places.localTip: Take the guided mangrove tour at low wind; you'll glide through tunnels of red mangroves teeming with wildlife.
places.story: Virginia Key was once Miami's segregation-era 'colored beach,' and is now a restored natural preserve. The outdoor center introduces paddlers to the mangrove ecosystems that protect Biscayne Bay.

places.name: South Beach Lifeguard Towers
places.reviewSummary: The pastel, Art Deco-inspired lifeguard towers dotting South Beach are among Miami's most photographed icons, each one a unique color scheme.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the colorful, whimsical towers against the ocean, with some noting the best ones require a walk along the sand.
places.localTip: The colorful tower near 10th/13th Streets shoots best in early-morning light with no crowds.
places.story: After Hurricane Andrew destroyed the old towers in 1992, artist William Lane designed whimsical replacements in candy colors and Deco shapes. They've become unofficial mascots of Miami Beach.
places.highlight: Don't miss the rainbow tower at dawn

places.name: Rickenbacker Causeway Viewpoint
places.reviewSummary: The causeway to Key Biscayne offers Miami's best skyline-over-water views, especially the pull-offs facing downtown. A classic sunset spot.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors call the skyline views spectacular, with some noting the toll and that pull-off parking fills at sunset.
places.localTip: Park at the Hobie Island pull-offs for the unobstructed downtown skyline; sunrise and blue hour are magic.
places.story: Opened in 1947, the Rickenbacker connects the mainland to the barrier islands and frames the city's skyline rising straight from Biscayne Bay. It's the photo every Miami visitor leaves with.
places.highlight: Don't miss blue hour over downtown

places.name: Wynwood Mural Streets
places.address: NW 2nd Ave & NW 25th St, Miami, FL 33127
places.reviewSummary: Block after block of free, ever-changing murals make Wynwood's streets an open-air photo studio. The most colorful walls in Miami.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the endless vibrant murals, with some noting popular walls get crowded and art changes often.
places.localTip: Walk NW 2nd Ave and the side streets early; the light is soft and you'll have the walls to yourself.
places.story: Beyond the ticketed Wynwood Walls, the entire neighborhood became a free canvas as artists from around the world painted its warehouses. The murals are repainted constantly, so no two visits look alike.

places.name: Bayfront Park & Skyline
places.reviewSummary: A 32-acre downtown bayfront park with fountains, a pier, and unobstructed views of the skyline and Biscayne Bay. Great for golden-hour shots.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors enjoy the bay views and green space downtown, with some noting it can feel busy during events.
places.localTip: The Pepper Fountain and the bayfront walk frame the skyline beautifully at sunset.
places.story: Designed in part by sculptor Isamu Noguchi in the 1980s, Bayfront Park is downtown's green front porch on the bay. Its Tina Hills Pavilion hosts the city's New Year's Eve fireworks.

places.name: Espanola Way String Lights
places.reviewSummary: The pastel Mediterranean facades and overhead string lights of Espanola Way make a dreamy, European-feeling photo backdrop.
places.googleReviewSummary: Photographers love the charming lane and lights at dusk, with some noting it's narrow and busy in the evenings.
places.localTip: Shoot at blue hour when the string lights glow against the pink-and-cream buildings.
places.story: This 1925 Spanish-village lane was designed to feel like the Mediterranean, and its restored facades and canopy lights still deliver. It's one of the most romantic photo corners in Miami Beach.
places.highlight: Don't miss the string lights at dusk

places.name: Cape Florida Lighthouse
places.reviewSummary: Miami's oldest standing structure, an 1825 lighthouse at the tip of Bill Baggs Cape Florida State Park, surrounded by palms and beach.
places.googleReviewSummary: Visitors love the historic lighthouse, beach, and views toward Stiltsville, with some noting the climb has limited tour times.
places.localTip: Climb the lighthouse on a clear morning for panoramic shots, then walk to the beach to glimpse the Stiltsville houses.
places.story: Built in 1825 and attacked during the Second Seminole War, the Cape Florida Lighthouse is the oldest structure in greater Miami. From its top you can see Stiltsville, the cluster of houses standing on pilings out in the bay.
places.highlight: Don't miss the view toward Stiltsville
